topic: EXP-127: Differential Refraction Eclipse Geometry Trap (Section 1.8.1)
status_note: CONTENT SUPERSEDED — Section 1.8.1 does not exist in the current sections.json. EXP-129 fully rewrote Section 1.8 as 'The Coordinate Collapse' (queue_id 61), removing the EXP-127 subsection. Per HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001, eclipse differential refraction arguments are to become a SEPARATE section (not under 1.8). This review examines the EXP-127 replacement_html content for quality, since it will likely be re-integrated elsewhere. All findings below apply to the EXP-127 content, not to any live site content.
our_argument_summary: EXP-127 argues that the dome's refraction fix (declaring the sun's 5,733 km altitude an optical illusion via aetheric medium n(r)) creates an unresolved differential refraction problem for the moon. Since the sun and moon are at different physical altitudes traversing different n(r) profiles, their apparent angular displacements differ. Eclipse geometry — which requires precise angular overlap — becomes indeterminate. The dome's own OPEN-007 (cited by EXP-127) allegedly acknowledges the moon's physical altitude is unknown. The argument concludes with a trilemma: keep straight-line geometry (contradicts refraction fix), compute ray-tracing through n(r) (impossible without known moon physics), or abandon the refraction fix (sun/firmament collision returns).

holes_found.description: FABRICATED CITATION: OPEN-007 does not exist on the dome's model page. The dome's raw-text/05-model.txt lists only OPEN-001 through OPEN-006 (with OPEN-002 and OPEN-005 resolved). There is no OPEN-007, OPEN-009, or OPEN-010. EXP-127 claims: 'OPEN-007 on the dome's own model page flags 2,534 km as optical/apparent — physical altitude unknown and labels the entire moon framework a structural problem.' This is false. Furthermore, this is a SYSTEMIC problem across our entire review: (1) WIN-051 detail_evidence says 'OPEN-007 addresses the unsolved problem of the moon's disappearance mechanism'; (2) WIN-059 detail_evidence says 'OPEN-007 concedes that polar shift dynamics have no working dome-native explanation'; (3) Section 9.6 says 'OPEN-007 notes that INTERMAGNET observatories report their positions in WGS84 coordinates.' These are THREE DIFFERENT claims about what the same nonexistent OPEN-007 says. If a dome defender checks their own model page and finds no OPEN-007, or finds our review cites it saying contradictory things in different places, our credibility is severely damaged. NOTE: Our raw text was captured 2026-03-12 from V50.6; the dome may have added OPEN-007+ in V51.0/V51.1, but the poller change log has no record of this, and regardless, our review contradicts itself about what OPEN-007 says.
holes_found.recommendation: URGENT: (a) Verify via the poller or a fresh scrape whether the dome's current site has OPEN-007, OPEN-009, OPEN-010. (b) If they do NOT exist, remove ALL references to OPEN-007 from wins.json (WIN-051, WIN-059), sections.json (Section 9.6), and the EXP-127 content. Replace with direct logical arguments: 'The dome has not addressed the moon's physical altitude under n(r)' rather than 'OPEN-007 says...' (c) If they DO exist, verify what they actually say and make all our references consistent. (d) This should be filed as a high-priority issue for the decider.

holes_found.description: WRONG n(r) VALUES: EXP-127 claims 'At the equatorial ring (r ≈ 14,105 km), n = 1.40; at the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), n = 3.49.' Both are incorrect. Using the dome's own formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1): at r = 14,105 km, n = 1.83 (not 1.40); at r = 20,015 km, n = 2.84 (not 3.49). The value n = 1.40 actually occurs at r ≈ 9,469 km (near the equator). Additionally, r = 14,105 km is NOT the 'equatorial ring' — the dome's equator maps to r ≈ 10,008 km. The 14,105 km value is the southern hemisphere reflection constant. The current Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse) uses the correct values. If EXP-127's content is re-integrated elsewhere, these numbers must be corrected.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the n(r) values with correct computations: equator (r ≈ 10,008 km) → n = 1.44; ice wall (r = 20,015 km) → n = 2.84. Also fix the 'equatorial ring' labeling — r = 14,105 km is the reflection parameter, not the equator.

holes_found.description: APPARENT vs PHYSICAL ALTITUDE ARGUMENT NOT ADEQUATELY SOURCED: The core claim that 'the moon's physical altitude is unknown' under n(r) is logically sound as an INFERENCE, but EXP-127 presents it as a dome ADMISSION (via the fabricated OPEN-007 cite). Without a dome admission, the argument structure changes: we are making the logical deduction that IF n(r) distorts the sun's apparent altitude, it must also distort the moon's. This is a strong argument — but it should be framed as our deduction, not as the dome's concession. A dome defender could counter: 'We never said the moon altitude is apparent; only the sun altitude was corrected because only the sun exceeds the firmament height. The moon at 2,534 km is well below the firmament at all radii, so no refraction correction is needed for the moon.' This counter is physically wrong (n(r) bends ALL light, not just light from objects that exceed the firmament), but EXP-127 doesn't preemptively address it.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe the moon altitude argument as a logical deduction from first principles rather than a dome admission. Add a preemptive counter to the likely defense: 'The dome may argue the refraction fix only applies to the sun, but Snell's law is not selective — a refractive medium bends all light passing through it.' Note that Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse) already makes this point well for Polaris light; the eclipse section should parallel that framing.

holes_found.description: TRILEMMA OPTION 3 IS WEAK: The trilemma includes 'Abandon the refraction fix — but then the sun/firmament collision returns.' This is true but trivially obvious and adds no analytical value. The dome clearly cannot abandon the refraction fix (that's established in Section 1.5/1.8). Including it makes the argument look like rhetorical padding. The real argument is a DILEMMA: (1) keep straight-line eclipse geometry and contradict the refraction fix, or (2) compute full ray-tracing (which the dome hasn't done and can't do without moon physics). The dome has no third option that resolves anything.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider reducing to a dilemma. If keeping the trilemma for completeness, at least acknowledge that option 3 is foreclosed and the real choice is between options 1 and 2.

holes_found.description: SCOPE OF DAMAGE OVERREACH ON TIDAL WINs: EXP-127 lists WIN-045/049/050/051 (tidal constituents) among affected WINs, claiming 'tidal forcing depends on physical moon altitude and mass, both unknown under n(r).' But tidal forcing in the dome model has never been derived from dome geometry anyway — the dome simply cites known tidal periods. The differential refraction argument adds no new weakness to the tidal WINs beyond what's already established (they're post-hoc adoptions of known values). Including them dilutes the stronger eclipse-specific argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Drop tidal WINs from the affected list or caveat them clearly: 'The tidal WINs are already assessed as post-hoc; the refraction issue adds a secondary concern about whether the dome could ever derive tidal forcing from dome geometry.' Focus the scope-of-damage section on WINs where differential refraction introduces a genuinely NEW problem (eclipse geometry, lunar magnetic coupling).

holes_found.description: REDUNDANCY WITH SECTION 1.8: The current Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse) already addresses the 3D ray-tracing problem, Snell's law universality, and the impossibility of a wavelength-selective medium. When EXP-127 is re-integrated as a separate section, it needs to avoid repeating these arguments and instead focus narrowly on the eclipse-specific geometry (physical shadow vs apparent alignment, the need for both physical positions to compute blocking geometry). The 'other side' section handles this well but the 'logical trap' section overlaps significantly with 1.8's existing argument.
holes_found.recommendation: When re-integrating, trim the general n(r) discussion (already in 1.8) and focus on: (a) the specific problem of eclipse blocking requiring PHYSICAL positions, (b) the dome's failure to specify the moon's physical position, (c) the impossibility of computing the shadow cone without ray-tracing.

stronger_arguments.description: The STRONGEST version of the differential refraction argument focuses on the distinction between APPARENT alignment (optical) and PHYSICAL blocking (shadow). Eclipses produce measurable physical effects (the dome claims magnetic anomalies, gravity effects, ionospheric changes). These effects depend on WHERE PHOTONS ARE PHYSICALLY BLOCKED — which requires physical positions and physical ray-tracing. Even if apparent alignment is coincidentally preserved, the physical shadow cone geometry changes under n(r). The dome's eclipse magnetic predictions (FSF formula) use shadow path geometry to scale station effects — if the shadow path shifts due to refraction, the scaling is wrong. This argument is already in EXP-127's 'other side' section but deserves promotion to the main argument.

The angular diameter argument is missing. The sun's angular diameter as seen from the ground is ~0.53° (32 arcminutes). For a total solar eclipse, the moon must subtend an angle at least this large from the observer's perspective. Under n(r), both the sun's and moon's apparent angular diameters change — and they change by different amounts because the objects are at different distances and different angles through the medium. If the moon's apparent angular diameter shrinks below the sun's under n(r), total eclipses become impossible — the model would predict only annular eclipses. This is a quantifiable, testable consequence of the refraction fix that EXP-127 misses entirely.
sources: Basic optics: angular magnification through a refractive medium depends on object distance and index gradient

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's recognition that aetheric refraction would shift apparent celestial positions is qualitatively correct physics — a refractive medium genuinely does shift apparent positions. The n(r) function is mathematically well-defined and produces computable values. If such a medium existed, differential refraction between objects at different heights would indeed be a real physical effect. The dome has also shown intellectual honesty in flagging 5,733 km as 'optical illusion due to aetheric refraction' rather than simply ignoring the sun/firmament collision.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Acknowledging the refraction creates more problems than it solves. A medium this strong (n = 1.08–2.84, vs Earth's atmosphere at 1.0003) produces effects throughout the cavity, not just for the sun. The dome has selectively applied it to one body while claiming predictions that require consistent treatment of all light through the same medium. The fix is cosmetically applied to avoid one collision while ignoring the cascading consequences.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender might argue: (1) The refraction fix is explicitly scoped to the SUN — the dome model only declares the sun's altitude an optical illusion because only the sun exceeds the firmament ceiling. The moon at 2,534 km is well below the firmament at every radius (H(r) > 2,534 for r < ~10,700 km, and the moon orbits at r ≈ 10,000 km where H ≈ 2,673 km — just barely above). Therefore the moon doesn't NEED a refraction correction, and 2,534 km can be treated as approximately physical. (2) Eclipse geometry has been empirically validated — the dome's eclipse timing predictions for 2024 (before they were suspended due to storm contamination) used standard shadow path geometry, and the dome would argue that empirical success validates the geometry even if the theoretical framework is incomplete. (3) The refraction medium might be designed to minimally affect near-overhead rays (the observer looks UP at the moon nearly overhead) while strongly bending grazing rays (the sun observed from far across the disc). A vertically stratified medium with horizontal gradient could produce this asymmetry.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Defense point (1) is the strongest — the moon IS below the firmament, so the 'fix' motivation doesn't apply to it. BUT: n(r) is a property of the MEDIUM, not of the object. Light from the moon traverses the same n(r) medium as light from the sun. Even if the moon doesn't need a position correction, its light is still refracted. At the equator (n = 1.44), moonlight arriving at ~15° elevation would be deflected by multiple degrees. The section should address this specific counter: 'The refraction fix is about the medium, not the object. Snell's law doesn't ask whether the source needs correcting — it bends all light according to the medium's index.' Also address point (3): the n(r) formula has no angular dependence — it's a scalar function of r, not of elevation angle.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No cross-WIN inconsistencies in the EXP-127 arguments. The eclipse WINs (010, 025, 068) all depend on shadow path geometry, and the argument that n(r) contaminates this geometry is consistent across all three. WIN-044 (FSF) is already Self-Contradicted for the firmament collision reason; the differential refraction argument adds an independent basis. Note: Section 1.8's Coordinate Collapse argument affects a broader set of WINs (all coordinate-dependent ones) and already subsumes most of EXP-127's impact. The eclipse-specific value-add of EXP-127 is narrow: the physical-shadow-vs-apparent-alignment distinction for eclipse WINs specifically.

claims_checked: n(r) at equatorial ring (r=14,105 km): EXP-127 claims 1.40
n(r) at ice wall (r=20,015 km): EXP-127 claims 3.49
Section 1.8 values: n=1.08 at r=3000, n=1.27 at Tropic of Cancer, n=1.44 at equator, n=2.84 at ice wall
quantitative_verification.computations: Used n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) with H(r) = 8537 × exp(−r/8619). Python verification: at r=14105 km, H=1662 km, n=1.827 (EXP-127 claims 1.40 — ERROR). At r=20015, H=837 km, n=2.840 (EXP-127 claims 3.49 — ERROR). n=1.40 actually occurs at r≈9469 km. Section 1.8's values all verified correct.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: EXP-127 has two quantitative errors: (1) n at r=14,105 km is 1.83, not 1.40 — a 31% error in the refractive index. (2) n at the ice wall is 2.84, not 3.49 — a 23% error. The r=14,105 km is also misidentified as the 'equatorial ring' (equator maps to r≈10,008 km). These errors don't invalidate the qualitative argument (n is still very large at all relevant radii) but would be noticed by any technically competent reader.

text_fingerprint.note: Content not currently live — EXP-127 replacement_html is the reviewed text. Fingerprint of EXP-127 replacement_html for tracking.
text_fingerprint.verdict: N/A — section content, not WIN verdict

deeper_analysis: The EXP-127 content makes a logically sound core argument — differential refraction between sun and moon under n(r) is a genuine consequence of the refraction fix — but it is undermined by three categories of problems: (1) CRITICAL: fabricated citations (OPEN-007/009/010), which is a systemic issue across our review and must be addressed urgently; (2) MAJOR: incorrect numerical values for n(r) that would be caught by any reader who checks the math; (3) STRUCTURAL: the content was designed as a subsection of 1.8 but has been displaced by the Coordinate Collapse rewrite and needs restructuring as a standalone section. The Coordinate Collapse argument (current Section 1.8) is actually STRONGER than EXP-127's eclipse argument because it affects the entire coordinate system rather than just eclipse geometry. EXP-127's unique contribution is narrow: the physical-shadow-vs-apparent-alignment distinction for eclipse predictions specifically. When re-integrated, the section should (a) strip all OPEN-007/009/010 references, (b) correct the n(r) values, (c) trim overlap with Section 1.8, and (d) focus on the shadow-geometry-requires-physical-positions argument, which is EXP-127's genuinely novel contribution.
